title: Linked List
summary: Linked-list problems are almost never about the data structure — they are **pointer choreography**: can you juggle 2–3 pointers without dropping a node, losing the head, or welding a cycle? A Microsoft OA staple, and the whole topic reduces to about five reusable maneuvers you can drill into muscle memory.

notes.heading: Pointer choreography — what these problems really test
notes.body:
A linked-list question is almost never about the data structure — it is about **pointer choreography**. The list is a thin excuse to test whether you can juggle two or three pointers without dropping a node, losing the head, or accidentally welding the list into a cycle. That is exactly why it is a **Microsoft interview staple**: it fits a short Codility slot and grades cleanly — either your pointers are right, or the list is visibly broken.

> [!ANALOGY]
> Picture the list as train cars coupled front-to-back on a track. You can only look forward, only walk forward, and each pointer variable is one hand holding one coupling. Every problem in this module is: re-couple the cars into a new order without letting any car roll away. A node no pointer is holding is *gone* — unreachable, leaked, unrecoverable.

Reach for the linked-list toolbox when you see:

- The input is a singly-linked list and you must **reverse**, **reorder**, **merge**, or **remove** nodes — anything that rewires `next` pointers.
- You need the **middle**, the **k-th from the end**, or to **detect/locate a cycle** in $O(1)$ extra space → fast/slow (tortoise & hare) pointers.
- The prompt says **in-place** or **$O(1)$ space** — that rules out copying to an array and forces pointer surgery.
- You must process **two sorted lists together** or splice sublists → dummy-node stitching.
- Phrases like *without knowing the length*, *one pass*, or *constant memory* on a list are dead giveaways for the two-pointer family.

> [!KEY]
> The whole topic reduces to about **five reusable maneuvers**: reverse a run of nodes, walk with a dummy, find the middle, race fast/slow, and stitch two lists together. Once those are muscle memory, most list problems fall in under five minutes — and the hard ones turn out to be these same primitives glued together.

notes.heading: The reversal primitive: save, flip, walk, walk
The dummy node: never special-case the head
Fast/slow pointers: the tortoise and the hare
Hard problems are easy primitives glued together
notes.body:
Here is the secret the Hard-tagged problems do not want you to know: they are almost never new algorithms. They are two or three of the maneuvers above, run in sequence:

- **Palindrome Linked List** = find middle + reverse the second half + walk two pointers comparing.
- **Reorder List** = find middle + reverse the second half + weave the halves alternately (Deep Dive 2).
- **Reverse Nodes in k-Group** = dummy + count-ahead + the reversal loop, once per block (Deep Dive 4).
- **Merge k Sorted Lists** = merge-two-lists, upgraded with a min-heap or divide-and-conquer.

> [!INTUITION]
> When a list problem looks scary, do not hunt for a clever algorithm — hunt for a **decomposition**. Ask: which of my five maneuvers, in what order, produces this transformation? Each phase is easy on its own; the *seams* between phases (splitting, re-stitching, terminating tails) are where you spend your care.

> [!EXAM]
> This decomposition is exactly what Microsoft-style Codility mediums reward. Reorder List is three easy functions in a trench coat. In a timed slot, write the plan as a comment first — `// 1. middle  2. reverse  3. weave` — then fill in each block. The comment doubles as your checklist for the final dry run.

notes.heading: Pitfalls, edge cases, and how to narrate
notes.body:
Five bugs account for nearly every wrong submission in this module:

- **Losing the rest of the list** — always stash `cur->next` in a temp *before* overwriting it. The #1 reversal bug.
- **Null dereference** — fast/slow guards must be `while (fast && fast->next)`, with `fast` checked first.
- **Unhandled head change** — if the first node can be deleted or moved, use a dummy. Forgetting it forces ugly `if (head == target)` special cases.
- **Wrong middle on even length** — pick your guard deliberately (see the table in the fast/slow section).
- **Accidental cycles** — after rewiring, some tail's `next` must end at `nullptr`, or you have built a loop.

**Edge cases to name out loud before coding:** empty list (`head == nullptr`), single node, two nodes, and — for k-group / nth-from-end — `k` or `n` equal to the full list length.

How to narrate in an interview (this scores points by itself):

1. Restate the binding constraint: *it says $O(1)$ space, so no array copy — pointer manipulation it is.*
2. Name the pattern: *this is find-middle plus reverse plus merge*, or *this is Floyd's algorithm.*
3. **Draw the pointers.** Sketch `prev / cur / nxt` boxes and move them by hand for one iteration — it catches off-by-ones and shows the interviewer your reasoning.
4. State complexity unprompted: nearly everything here is $O(n)$ time, $O(1)$ space — say so.

> [!WARNING]
> An accidental cycle is the sneakiest failure mode because *nothing crashes* — traversal just never ends and the judge reports **TLE**, not a wrong answer. If a list solution times out, suspect a rewiring step that never terminated a tail with `nullptr` before you suspect your complexity.

> [!EXAM]
> Before submitting, dry-run your final code on a **two-node list**. That size exposes most pointer bugs — head handling, null guards, tail termination — in about 30 seconds. Cheapest insurance you will ever buy in an OA.

problems.name: Middle of the Linked List
problems.note: Fast/slow pointers — the cleanest possible introduction; `slow` lands on the middle.

problems.name: Reverse Linked List
problems.note: The reversal primitive — know it iteratively **and** recursively.

problems.name: Linked List Cycle
problems.note: Floyd detection: do `fast` and `slow` ever meet?

problems.name: Merge Two Sorted Lists
problems.note: Dummy-node stitching of two lists — build the output off `dummy.next`.

problems.name: Remove Duplicates from Sorted List
problems.note: Unlink adjacent equal nodes; only advance `cur` after the value truly changes.

problems.name: Remove Nth Node From End of List
problems.note: Two pointers a gap of `n` apart; the dummy handles removing the head itself.

problems.name: Palindrome Linked List
problems.note: Find middle, reverse the second half, compare — the $O(1)$-space composition.

problems.name: Linked List Cycle II
problems.note: Floyd part 2: find the *entry* node of the cycle (see Deep Dive 1).

problems.name: Odd Even Linked List
problems.note: Weave two sublists by index parity, then join odd-tail to even-head.

problems.name: Reorder List
problems.note: Middle + reverse + merge — three primitives in one problem (see Deep Dive 2).

problems.name: Copy List with Random Pointer
problems.note: Interleave-clone trick for $O(1)$ extra space (see Deep Dive 3).

problems.name: Add Two Numbers
problems.note: Dummy node + carry propagation across two lists of digits.

problems.name: Reverse Nodes in k-Group
problems.note: Reverse fixed-size blocks and re-stitch them cleanly (see Deep Dive 4).

problems.name: Merge k Sorted Lists
problems.note: Min-heap or divide-and-conquer merge — also drills heaps, a gap of yours.

deepdives.q: **Linked List Cycle II (Floyd's algorithm)** — A list may contain a cycle. Detect it and return the exact node where the cycle *begins*, using $O(1)$ extra space.
**Reorder List** — Rearrange `1→2→3→4→5` into `1→5→2→4→3` (first, last, second, second-last, …) in-place, $O(1)$ extra space.
**Copy List with Random Pointer** — Deep-copy a list where each node has a `next` pointer and a `random` pointer to an arbitrary node (or null), using $O(1)$ extra space beyond the output.
**Reverse Nodes in k-Group** — Reverse every consecutive block of `k` nodes; if fewer than `k` nodes remain at the end, leave them untouched.

quiz.q: You must **delete the k-th node from the end** of a singly-linked list in *one pass* and $O(1)$ space. Which technique fits?
options: Reverse the list, delete the k-th node from the front, then reverse back.
Store every node in a hash map keyed by index, then jump to index `len - k`.
Two pointers a gap of `k` apart, advanced together until the front one hits the end.
Fast/slow pointers where fast moves two steps per one step of slow.
quiz.explanation: The gap trick: give the front pointer a `k`-step head start, then move both at the same speed; when the front hits the end, the back pointer sits just before the target — one pass, no extra memory. Reversing twice is three passes; the hash map is $O(n)$ space; fast-at-2x finds the *middle*, not the k-th from the end.

quiz.q: In the reversal loop, you accidentally write `cur->next = prev;` **before** `ListNode* nxt = cur->next;`. What actually happens?
options: `nxt` now stores `prev`, so the unvisited suffix is lost and the loop terminates early with a mostly-missing list.
Nothing — pointers are copied by value, so the order of the two lines is irrelevant.
Immediate segfault on the first iteration for any non-empty list.
It only breaks on even-length lists.
quiz.explanation: Flipping first clobbers the only reference to the rest of the list: `nxt` then reads the *already-flipped* pointer (i.e. `prev`), so `cur` walks backwards/stops instead of forward. On `1→2→3` you would return just `1` with `2→3` leaked. No crash, no length-dependence — just silent data loss, which is why *save, flip, walk, walk* order matters.

quiz.q: Why must the fast/slow guard be `while (fast && fast->next)` rather than just `while (fast->next)`?
options: No reason — `fast` can never become null inside the loop.
On even-length lists `fast` ends up as `nullptr`, so `fast->next` would dereference null and crash.
Without it, `slow` overshoots the middle by one on odd-length lists.
The shorter guard makes the loop infinite.
quiz.explanation: With an even number of nodes, `fast = fast->next->next` eventually lands `fast` on `nullptr`. The next guard evaluation would compute `nullptr->next` — undefined behavior. Checking `fast` first exploits short-circuit evaluation to bail out safely. Odd lists happen to be fine, which makes this bug extra sneaky: it passes half your mental tests.

quiz.q: Running the standard reversal loop on `1→2→3`: after exactly **two** iterations, what are `prev` and `cur`?
options: `prev` = node 1 (list `1→∅`), `cur` = node 2
`prev` = node 3 (list `3→2→1`), `cur` = `nullptr`
`prev` = node 2 with `2→3` still intact, `cur` = node 3
`prev` = node 2 (list `2→1→∅`), `cur` = node 3
quiz.explanation: Iteration 1: stash `nxt=2`, flip `1→∅`, so `prev=1, cur=2`. Iteration 2: stash `nxt=3`, flip `2→1`, so `prev=2, cur=3` — and the reversed prefix reads `2→1→∅`. Option C forgets that the flip already happened; option B describes the state after *three* iterations (the full reversal).

quiz.q: Reorder List solved as *find middle → reverse second half → weave alternately*. Total complexity?
options: $O(n)$ time, $O(1)$ space
$O(n \log n)$ time, $O(1)$ space
$O(n)$ time, $O(n)$ space
$O(n^2)$ time, $O(1)$ space
quiz.explanation: Each phase is a single linear walk: middle is $O(n)$, reversing half is $O(n)$, weaving is $O(n)$ — three passes is still $O(n)$, and every phase only uses a constant number of pointer variables. $O(n^2)$ is what the naive *fetch the last node repeatedly* approach costs; $O(n)$ space is the vector-of-pointers shortcut this decomposition exists to avoid.

quiz.q: In Floyd's algorithm, after `slow` and `fast` collide you reset one pointer to `head` and advance **both by one step**. Why do they meet exactly at the cycle's entry?
options: Because the collision point is always the entry node itself.
Because `slow` has gone around the cycle exactly once when they collide.
Because head→entry distance `a` equals `k·c − b`, which is exactly the meeting point's forward distance to the entry.
Because `fast` moves twice as fast, it is forced to stop at the entry.
quiz.explanation: From *fast travelled twice slow's distance*: $2(a+b)=a+b+k\cdot c$, so $a = k\cdot c - b$. Walking $a$ steps from the head reaches the entry; walking $a = k\cdot c - b$ steps forward from the meeting point also lands on the entry (it completes the laps). The collision point is generally *not* the entry (A), and neither pointer counts laps at runtime — the equation does the work.

quiz.q: In which of these situations can you safely **skip the dummy node**?
options: Deleting every node whose value equals `x` — the head might match.
Finding the middle node of the list.
Merging two sorted lists into one output list.
Removing the n-th node from the end, where `n` may equal the length.
quiz.explanation: Finding the middle does zero rewiring — the head cannot change, so there is nothing for a dummy to protect. In every other option the first node can be deleted or the output head is unknown until runtime, which is precisely the dummy's job: give the head a `prev` and return `dummy.next` at the end.

m05-reverse-step.caption: Mid-reversal snapshot: nodes **1** and **2** already point backwards (accent), `cur` is about to flip node **3**, and `nxt` keeps a grip on node **4** so the suffix is never lost.

m05-dummy-delete.caption: Deleting the **head** with a dummy in front: the head has a `prev` like every other node, so the ordinary bypass `prev->next = cur->next` (green arc) works — no special case. Return `dummy.next`.

m05-fastslow-middle.caption: Fast/slow race on `1→2→3→4→5`: after step *t*, `slow` is at node *t+1* and `fast` at node *2t+1* (labels s0/f0, s1, s2/f1, f2). When `fast` can no longer take two steps, `slow` stands on the **middle**.

</svg>

m05-floyd-entry.caption: Floyd phase 2: `a` = head→entry, `b` = entry→meeting point (red). Since $a = k\cdot c - b$, walking one pointer from **head** and one from **meet**, both one step at a time, makes them collide exactly at the **entry** node.

m05-interleave-clone.caption: The interleaving clone: every copy `A'` sits right after its original `A`, so `original->next` **is** the clone. The clone's random comes for free: `A'.random = A.random->next` (bottom arc mirrors the top arc, one `->next` later).
m05-interleave-clone.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 660 250" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Clones interleaved into the original list so random pointers can be copied without a hash map">
<text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">clones woven in: A→A'→B→B'→C→C'</text>
<path d="M 62 94 Q 250 30 456 92" class="f-none s-accent2" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent2)"/>
<text x="480" y="50" class="t-sm t-accent2">A.random = C</text>
<rect x="40" y="100" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/>
